structuredFacts.title: Mona Lisa
structuredFacts.subtitle: Painting by Leonardo da Vinci
structuredFacts.leadParagraph:
The Mona Lisa is a half-length portrait painting by the Italian artist Leonardo da Vinci. Considered an archetypal masterpiece of the Italian Renaissance, it has been described as "the best known, the most visited, the most written about, the most sung about, [and] the most parodied work of art in the world." The painting's novel qualities include the subject's enigmatic expression, the monumentality of the composition, the subtle modelling of forms, and the atmospheric illusionism.
The painting has been traditionally considered to depict the Italian noblewoman Lisa del Giocondo. It is painted in oil on a white poplar panel. Leonardo never gave the painting to the Giocondo family. It was believed to have been painted between 1503 and 1506; however, Leonardo may have continued working on it as late as 1517. King Francis I of France acquired the Mona Lisa after Leonardo's death in 1519, and it became the property of the French Republic. It has normally been on display at the Louvre in Paris since 1797.
The painting's global fame and popularity partly stem from its 1911 theft by Vincenzo Peruggia, who attributed his actions to Italian patriotism—a belief it should belong to Italy. The theft and subsequent recovery in 1914 generated unprecedented publicity for an art theft, and led to the publication of many cultural depictions such as the 1915 opera Mona Lisa, two early 1930s films (The Theft of the Mona Lisa and Arsène Lupin), and the song "Mona Lisa" recorded by Nat King Cole—one of the most successful songs of the 1950s.
The Mona Lisa is one of the most valuable paintings in the world. It holds the Guinness World Record for the highest known painting insurance valuation in history at US$100 million in 1962, equivalent to $1 billion as of 2023.
structuredFacts.briefSummary: Painted in oil on a white poplar panel, Lisa del Giocondo was definitively identified in 2005 thanks to a marginal note by Agostino Vespucci. Although commissioned to paint her portrait, Leonardo da Vinci kept the masterpiece until his death rather than delivering it to her family. King Francis I of France acquired the work in 1519, preserving a canvas that modern high-resolution scans show originally featured eyebrows and eyelashes. While always admired, the painting saw its global fame explode after Vincenzo Peruggia stole it from the Louvre in 1911. By 1962, this renown culminated in an insurance valuation of $100 million, the highest ever assessed for a painting.

cards.title: The Woman Behind the Smile
cards.summary: When Francesco del Giocondo commissioned Leonardo da Vinci to paint a portrait of his wife, Lisa, in 1503, nobody could foresee its legacy. Rendered in oil on a white poplar panel, the artwork depicts Lisa del Giocondo in serene detail. Her true identity remained a subject of debate until 2005, when a marginal note by Agostino Vespucci definitively confirmed who she was. Rather than handing over the work, Leonardo chose to keep the unfinished masterpiece by his side until his death, denying the family their portrait. By holding onto the canvas, the artist ensured the Giocondo family would never receive their original commission. Ultimately, what began as a private Florentine request transformed into Leonardo's lifelong, undelivered obsession.
cards.readerQuestion: How did a 16th-century Florentine portrait begin, and who was the woman behind the famous smile?
cards.keyTakeaway: A private Florentine commission became Leonardo's lifelong, undelivered masterpiece.

cards.title: Leonardo's Living Canvas
cards.summary: Leonardo da Vinci pioneered a reserved posture, three-quarter profile, and the soft, outline-free sfumato technique . He sought to revolutionize portraiture by blending scientific observation of light with psychological depth . The masterpiece utilizes an imaginary landscape background with aerial perspective and a mediating loggia . This integration of sfumato and elevated scenery established a new standard for Renaissance portraiture . Modern high-resolution scans revealed that the subject originally had eyebrows and eyelashes . Ultimately, Leonardo's innovative techniques redefined portraiture and captured lifelike human expression .
cards.readerQuestion: What revolutionary artistic techniques did Leonardo employ to bring his subject to life?
cards.keyTakeaway: Leonardo's innovative techniques redefined portraiture and captured lifelike expression.

cards.title: The Royal French Acquisition
cards.summary: In 1519, King Francis I of France acquired the painting following the death of Leonardo da Vinci. This acquisition initiated the masterpiece's transition into the French royal collection. Within the background landscape, researcher Silvano Vincenti identified a bridge as the Etruscan-Roman Romito di Laterina bridge near Arezzo, Italy. This background detail continues to spark historical research today, long after the painting transitioned from private royal ownership to a public home. In 1797, the artwork was placed on permanent public display at the Louvre Museum in Paris. By securing the masterpiece, French royalty ultimately cemented its permanent Parisian home.
cards.readerQuestion: How did the Italian masterpiece transition into the hands of French royalty and find its home in the Louvre?
cards.keyTakeaway: French royalty secured the masterpiece, cementing its permanent Parisian home.

cards.title: The Great Louvre Heist
cards.summary: Slipping the Mona Lisa under his coat, Vincenzo Peruggia quietly walked out of the Louvre in August 1911. The Italian handyman was driven by the conviction that the masterpiece belonged in his homeland rather than in France. For nearly three years, the painting remained missing until its dramatic recovery in 1914. This sensational return sparked a wave of international media coverage and captured the public imagination. Interestingly, the famous title 'Mona' is nothing more than an Italian contraction meaning 'my lady'. Ultimately, it was this daring museum heist that transformed a quiet Renaissance portrait into a global icon.
cards.readerQuestion: How did a daring museum theft transform a Renaissance masterpiece into a global sensation?
cards.keyTakeaway: A daring 1911 theft sparked the painting's global fame.

cards.title: The Price of Mystery
cards.summary: In 1962, the Mona Lisa secured the Guinness World Record for the highest painting insurance valuation in history at $100 million. This massive financial appraisal was driven by the portrait's unprecedented global fame and enduring mystique. Adjusted for inflation, that historic figure equates to approximately $1 billion today. Such staggering worth cemented the masterpiece as the most valuable and debated artwork on Earth. Yet, while its value is clear, alternative theories still suggest other noblewomen or even Leonardo da Vinci himself as the true subject. Ultimately, the painting remains an invaluable, mysterious icon of global culture.
cards.readerQuestion: What is the modern legacy of the painting, and what mysteries still surround its subject?

briefSummaryProvenance.fact: Chapter 2: High-resolution scans by modern technology revealed that the subject originally had eyebrows and eyelashes.

briefSummaryProvenance.sentence: Yet, its global fame exploded after Vincenzo Peruggia stole the panel from the Louvre in 1911 .
briefSummaryProvenance.fact: Chapter 4: The painting's global fame is largely attributed to its 1911 theft by Vincenzo Peruggia, who hid the panel under his coat to walk out of the Louvre.

briefSummaryProvenance.sentence: By 1962, the masterpiece held the highest painting insurance valuation in history at $100 million .
briefSummaryProvenance.fact: Chapter 5: The Mona Lisa holds the Guinness World Record for the highest known painting insurance valuation in history, assessed at $100 million in 1962.
